data.title: Western Digital rises after 'strong' Q4; driven by 'strength in datacenter sales,' say analysts
data.description:
Shares of Western Digital (NASDAQ:WDC) rose about 6% on Thursday after fiscal fourth quarter results and first quarter outlook surpassed estimates, drawing positive views from analysts.

Wedbush kept its Outperform rating on Western Digital and raised the price target on the stock to $90 from $78.

"For the 2nd quarter in a row; WDC reported strong results and provided a better than consensus (but we also believe very beatable) outlook," said analysts led by Matt Bryson.

Specifically, the analysts said that Western Digital is seemingly set to benefit from continued favorable mix shift (driven in particular by the ramp of its industry leading 26TB CMR/32TB UltraSMR drives) amidst an extremely favorable industry backdrop (with supply trailing demand).

The analysts believe the company's gross margin, or GM, guidance (up 20 basis points at the midpoint) in particular looks extremely conservative, seemingly setting the company up for anotherbeat and raise quarter.

Bryson and his team noted that they do not see any reason to shift their constructive view on Western Digital.

Benchmark Equity Research maintained its Buy rating on Western Digital and increased the price target to $85 from $58.

"Driven by continued strength in datacenter sales Western Digital posted upside results for the June quarter and guided higher for the September quarter," said analyst Mark Miller.

The analysts added that Cloud sales represented 90% of total June quarter sales up from 87% in the prior quarter. Nearline drive shipments were up 17% sequentially and 36% year-over-year. The analysts see fiscal year 2026 non-GAAP earnings growing 23% year-over-year to $6.92 per diluted share.

Morgan Stanley kept its Overweight rating on Western Digital and upped the price target to $92 from $83.

Analysts led by Erik Woodring said Western Digital results re-confirm Hard Disk Drive, or HDD, demand strength and supply tightness, helping to sustain stable pricing and elongate the HDD cycle.

The analysts expect some nitpicks on September quarter gross margin guidance, but added that the company management is likely being conservative, leaving open the potential for stronger EPS upside than modeled.

Woodring and his team noted that positive estimate revisions, a structural re-rating of HDD stocks, and closing the valuation discount with Seagate Technology (STX) are all likely to drive Western Digital's outperformance.

data.title: Moderna gets EU nod for updated COVID-19 vaccine
data.description:
• Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA) has received approval from the European Commission for an updated version of its COVID-19 vaccine, Spikevax.
  • This updated vaccine targets the LP.8.1 variant and is intended for individuals aged six months and older to help prevent COVID-19.
  • The approval aligns with recommendations from global health organizations, which suggested updating COVID-19 vaccines to address the LP.8.1 strain for the upcoming 2025-2026 vaccination season.
  • This authorization is applicable in all 27 EU member countries, along with Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway.
  • Doses will be ready for eligible groups before the 2025-2026 vaccination season begins, the company said.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: META, MSFT, MPW, and more
data.description:
Stock futures ticked higher on Thursday buoyed by blockbuster tech earnings that helped offset Fed signals casting doubt on a potential September rate cut, even as June’s year-over-year PCE data came in slightly above expectations.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) +12% - Shares surged after the company blew past Q2 earnings expectations, reporting a 22% Y/Y revenue jump to $47.5B and a 36% rise in net income to $18.3B. Despite ramping up AI spending, Meta significantly boosted its operating margin to 43% and provided a robust Q3 revenue outlook of $47.5B–$50.5B, exceeding analyst forecasts of $46.3B.
  • Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) +9% - Shares climbed after the company reported upbeat FQ4 results, largely driven by continued strong growth in its cloud and artificial intelligence segment, with Intelligent Cloud revenue surging 26%. Looking ahead, Microsoft's optimistic 1Q '26 revenue forecast of $75.25B (midpoint) comfortably exceeds consensus estimates of $74.15B, with Azure's growth alone expected to surge by 37% in 1H2026. The tech giant also anticipates robust capital expenditures exceeding $30B in FQ1, signaling ongoing heavy investment to meet strong demand.
  • Western Digital (NASDAQ:WDC) +8% – Shares rose after the data storage firm beat FQ4 estimates and issued an upbeat FQ1 outlook. Q4 non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.66 vs. $1.48 consensus, with revenue up 30% Y/Y to $2.61B, topping the $2.47B estimate. For FQ1, the company guided to EPS of $1.54 and revenue of $2.7B at the midpoint, both above Street expectations. Western Digital also declared a $0.10/share quarterly dividend and emphasized the continued relevance of HDDs in an AI-driven future.
  • Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) +21% – Shares soared after the AI data center operator posted a narrower-than-expected Q4 loss of $0.03/share vs. the estimated loss of $0.16, with revenue of $38M topping forecasts. Momentum accelerated after partner CoreWeave exercised a lease option for an additional 150 MW, bringing total contracted lease revenue to $11B. Management highlighted strong AI infrastructure demand and ongoing efforts to market a multi-gigawatt pipeline. The board continues to review strategic alternatives for its cloud business.
  • Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW) +6% — Shares gained after the hospital REIT reported stronger-than-expected Q2 results, with a normalized FFO of $0.14 per share beating the $0.13 consensus. While tenant bankruptcies, including Prospect Medical, drove a $0.16 net loss due to $111M in impairment charges, revenue of $240.4M topped expectations and rose sequentially. Rental income from new operators rose sharply to $11M from $3.4M in Q1, with CEO Edward Aldag expressing confidence in reaching $1B in annualized pro rata cash rent by Q4 2026.
  • CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) +5% — Shares rose after the company exceeded Q2 expectations, with revenue climbing 9% Y/Y to $98.9B, surpassing consensus by $4.33B. Strong performance in its Health Care Benefits segment (+12% Y/Y to $36.2B) and Pharmacy & Consumer Wellness division offset weakness in Health Services. Despite a 30 bps rise in the medical benefit ratio to 89.9%, adjusted EPS of $1.81 beat estimates by $0.35. CVS also raised its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $6.30–$6.40 (vs. $6.12 est), defying industry concerns around rising medical costs.

Biggest stock losers

  • Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) -5% – Shares slipped even after the chipmaker topped FQ3 earnings and revenue estimates and issued stronger-than-expected guidance for the current quarter. For FQ4, Qualcomm expects adjusted EPS of $2.75–$2.95 (midpoint $2.85) on revenue of $10.3B–$11.1B (midpoint $10.7B), both above analyst estimates of $2.82 EPS on $10.61B revenue.
  • AB InBev (BUD) -11% – Shares plunged after the brewer reported a steeper-than-expected 1.9% drop in Q2 volumes, missing forecasts for a 0.3% decline, despite gains in revenue and profit. The slump was driven by weakness in China, where volumes fell 7.4%, and the company admitted it was underperforming the industry as well as Brazil, due to tough comparisons.
  • Sirius XM Holdings (NASDAQ:SIRI) -10% — Shares declined after the company posted mixed Q2 results. While SiriusXM reported a narrower self-pay subscriber decline of 68,000 (improved by 32,000 Y/Y) and a stable churn rate of 1.5%, total subscribers stood at ~33M. The trial funnel expanded slightly to 7.6M. Despite the subscriber drop, the company reaffirmed its full-year 2025 outlook, including $8.5B in revenue, $2.6B in adjusted EBITDA, and $1.15B in free cash flow.

data.title: Exxon set to sign offshore gas exploration deal with Libya. embassy says
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) is on the verge of signing an offshore gas exploration deal in Libya, according to the U.S. Embassy in Tripoli, which would mark the company's return to the country after a decade-long absence.

Separately, the Libyan government has launched its first major oil and gas licensing round since 2007, offering 22 onshore and offshore exploration blocks.

The head of Libya's National Oil Corporation said nearly 40 multinational companies, including Exxon (NYSE:XOM), Chevron (CVX), TotalEnergies (TTE) and Eni (E) have shown interest in bidding for blocks.

Libya currently produces 1.3M-1.4M bbl/day of oil, but the NOC aims to ramp up output to 2M bbl/day within the next three years, provided sufficient foreign investment is secured, and Libya could hold more than 90B barrels of untapped oil and gas reserves, positioning the country as one of the most promising yet underexplored energy frontiers in North Africa.

Key have begun to resume operations in Libya: BP and Eni restarted drilling activities in the Ghadames Basin in 2024, and oilfield services company Weatherford returned in 2025 after more than a decade-long absence.
